name: Hyrox Peak & Taper — ACWR-Corrected
goal: Event readiness for Hyrox on April 29. Bring ACWR from 1.5 back below 1.3 before adding race-specific stimulus. Maintain Fitbod strength scores above 58. Arrive fresh and race-ready.
notes:
CRITICAL: ACWR is at 1.5 — well above the 1.3 injury-risk threshold. The first phase of this plan is deliberately conservative to let acute load settle before the key simulation session. Today's recovery markers are excellent (HRV 130ms, 8.75h sleep), so the body is absorbing recent load well, but we must avoid stacking more before the ratio corrects.

NO CHANGES FROM EXISTING PLAN: The current program structure is sound. ACWR management in Phase 1 is correct, the simulation placement on April 18 gives 11 days to race day (ideal for supercompensation), and the taper timeline aligns with the prep notes. Recovery data today (HRV 130ms) confirms the body is handling load well — the plan's conservative start is the right call to let the ratio self-correct rather than digging a deeper hole.

TIME TRIAL SKIPPED: With ACWR at 1.5, a 5k TT is unnecessary injury risk. Use the April 11 simulation data instead — 1km intervals at 5:16/km (HR 159–160, RPE 5) gives a solid race-pace reference. Target 5:30–6:00/km for race day 1km legs given you'll be doing 8 of them with stations between. The April 18 full simulation will provide the best predictor of race-day performance.

RACE DAY NUTRITION: Eat a familiar, carb-rich meal 2–3 hours before start. Sip water in the hour before. Have a gel or fast carb source available for after station 4–5 if energy drops.

WEIGHT TREND: +1.7kg over 7 days is tracking well toward 75kg goal. Maintain caloric surplus through the sharpen phase, then eat to maintenance during taper — prioritise carb loading the final 2–3 days.

STATION ORDER: Ski Erg → Sled Push → Sled Pull → Burpee Broad Jumps → Rowing → Farmer's Carry → Sandbag Lunges → Wall Balls. Wall Balls are the biggest energy cost — save something for them.

ACWR PROJECTION: ATL 137.6, CTL 91.4. With rest today and light sessions Mon/Tue, the heavy April 6–11 block (strength + strength + Z2 + simulation) will age out of the 7-day acute window by April 13–14. Estimated ACWR by simulation day (April 18): ~1.1–1.2 assuming compliance with the load management phase. This puts you right in the optimal window for the key session.

phases.name: Phase 1 — Load Management
phases.focus: Bring ACWR from 1.5 back below 1.3 before the key simulation. Reduce acute training load with lighter sessions and extra rest. Maintain strength scores with one upper push and one lower posterior chain session. Strict Zone 2 HR discipline on the one easy run. By the end of this phase, the heavy April 6–11 block will have fully exited the 7-day acute window.

days.session: Full rest day. ACWR is 1.5 — the single most important thing today is NOT training. Walk up to 30 min if desired, but no structured exercise. Recovery markers are excellent (HRV 130ms, 8.75h sleep), so the body is handling load well, but the ratio must drop before we add more. Hydrate, eat well, stretch or foam roll if needed.

Upper body push focus: chest, shoulders, triceps. ~40 min. Bench press, OHP, dips or close-grip bench, lateral raises. Moderate volume — aim to maintain push score ≥62 but do not push to failure. RPE 6–7 on working sets. Upper body carries low systemic fatigue compared to lower, which is why this goes here instead of legs. Save legs for Thursday. Log Fitbod scores post-session.

Full rest. No training. Walk OK. Focus on sleep — aim for 8+ hours. This is the second rest day in four days, which is unusual, but necessary. ACWR should be approaching 1.3 by now as the heavy April 6–11 block ages out of the acute window. The April 6 strength session (TRIMP contribution) drops out of the 7-day window today.

25 min at STRICT 120–130 bpm. This is heart rate controlled — not pace controlled. Based on your April 8 data (avg HR 134), you were ~4 bpm high. Expect to run ~9:00–9:30/km or slower. If HR drifts above 130, walk until it drops below 125, then resume. Log average HR and pace — this is your true Zone 2 benchmark. Purpose: active recovery and aerobic maintenance without spiking acute load.

days.focus: Rest — Leg day rescheduled to Friday
days.session: Rest day. Leg session moved to Friday due to scheduling. Walk OK. Focus on sleep and nutrition.

days.focus: Strength — Lower Body, posterior chain focus (Fitbod)
days.session: Lower body — posterior chain focus: RDLs, hamstring curls, hip thrusts, calf raises. ~45 min. Moderate load — aim for RPE 6–7 on working sets. Keep leg score ≥60. This is the LAST significant lower body strength session before Hyrox. Do not go heavy or to failure. The goal is to maintain neuromuscular readiness without creating soreness that interferes with Sunday's simulation. If you have any doubt about recovery in 48 hours, reduce volume by one set per exercise. Avoid quad-dominant movements (squats, leg press) — the simulation run legs will handle quad stimulus on Sunday.

Full rest. This is your pre-simulation recovery day. Walk, stretch, foam roll. Eat a larger meal with extra carbohydrates at dinner — you're fuelling for tomorrow's simulation. Lay out your kit. Review the station order and your pacing plan: 5:30–6:00/km run legs, conservative starts on ski erg and rower, rhythm on burpee broad jumps, break wall balls into manageable sets. Aim for 8+ hours sleep. Legs should feel fresh and ready.

phases.name: Phase 2 — Race Simulation & Sharpen
phases.focus: Execute the key race simulation at 70% effort to establish a baseline and build race-day confidence. Follow with recovery and one upper-body maintenance session to preserve Fitbod scores heading into the taper. Final Zone 2 run to close out meaningful training.

days.session:
Full Hyrox race format at 70% effort. This is the most important session in the entire program — 11 days before race day, ideal for supercompensation. Structure: 8 × (1km run + station) in official order.

Run legs: target 5:30–6:00/km — deliberately slower than your 5:16 interval pace since you're doing 8 of them with stations between. Walk 2–3 min between run+station if needed at 70% effort.

Station order and cues:
(1) Ski Erg 1000m — pace conservatively, don't blow up early. Aim for a 2:00–2:10/500m split.
(2) Sled Push 50m — drive from hips, steady and controlled. Short powerful steps.
(3) Sled Pull 50m — hand-over-hand, sit back and use bodyweight. Controlled rhythm.
(4) Burpee Broad Jumps 80m — sustainable rhythm. This is where HR spikes hardest. Don't rush.
(5) Rowing 1000m — hold a steady 500m split (~2:00–2:10), don't sprint the first 500m.
(6) Farmer's Carry 200m — grip and posture, don't rush. Squeeze the handles hard, shoulders back.
(7) Sandbag Lunges 100m — core tight, controlled steps, conserve energy for wall balls. Don't overstride.
(8) Wall Balls 100 reps — biggest energy cost. Break into sets of 15–20 with 5–10 sec rest. Breathe at the top of each rep.

Log: total elapsed time, each 1km run split, RPE per station (1–10), overall RPE, max HR. This data becomes your race-day pacing plan.

Substitutions if equipment unavailable: ski erg → assault bike 50 cal, sled push/pull → heavy prowler or loaded farmer's carry 100m, sandbag lunges → walking lunges with dumbbells (20–24kg each).

Cool down: 5 min walk. Stretch hamstrings, quads, hip flexors, shoulders. Eat within 60 min — high carb + protein (aim 40g+ protein, 80g+ carbs).

Full rest. Do not train. Walk up to 20 min if desired. The simulation is the highest-load session in this plan and your body needs to absorb it. Foam roll any sore areas — expect quads, glutes, grip, and shoulders to be the main fatigue sites. Eat at a surplus — prioritise protein (1.6g/kg = ~113g) and carbohydrates for glycogen replenishment. Aim for 8+ hours sleep. Note which stations felt hardest and which muscle groups were unexpectedly fatigued — this informs race-day strategy.

Upper body pull focus: barbell or dumbbell rows, pull-ups (aim toward your PR of 9 reps but don't max out), face pulls, bicep curls. ~40 min. Moderate volume at RPE 6–7 — do not go to failure. This is the last upper body session before the event. Aim to maintain pull score ≥60. Purpose: maintain pulling strength relevant for sled pull and rowing, and keep overall Fitbod score above 58 through the taper. Log scores.

phases.name: Phase 3 — Taper
phases.focus: Arrive at Hyrox fresh, confident, and fueled. Volume cut 30–40% from normal. No new training stimulus. Brief touches of race-specific movement to stay sharp without creating fatigue. Priorities: sleep 8+ hours every night, nutrition (carb-load final 48–72 hours), hydration (clear urine throughout the day), and mental preparation. No strength training — Fitbod scores will hold for 7 days without stimulus.

days.session: 20 min at 120–130 bpm. Easy shakeout. Legs should feel light after two days of recovery post-simulation. No pace targets — HR governs everything. If anything feels off (unusual soreness, fatigue, heavy legs), cut to 15 min or walk instead. Purpose: blood flow, maintaining running rhythm, and mental confidence that the legs still work. This is the last run over 15 min before race day.

20–25 min at 50% effort. This is mental rehearsal, not physical stimulus. Touch each movement pattern briefly: Ski erg 250m easy, Rower 250m easy, 5 wall balls, Sled push 15m light (or 10 goblet squats if no sled), 25m farmer's carry, 10 bodyweight lunges. Between each: walk and visualise the race-day transition — jog into the station, set up quickly, find your rhythm, finish and move to the next run leg. Think through your nutrition plan (meal 2–3 hrs before, gel at station 4–5 if needed) and hydration. You should finish feeling confident and mentally sharp, not physically taxed.

Full rest. No training. Light walk OK (15–20 min max). Begin carb-loading today — increase carbohydrate intake to 6–8g/kg bodyweight (~425–565g). Focus on familiar, easy-to-digest sources: rice, pasta, bread, potatoes, fruit, oats. Maintain protein at ~1.6g/kg (~113g). Hydrate consistently — aim for clear urine. Sleep 8+ hours. Continue foam rolling if any residual tightness.

15 min total — very light, just enough to prime movement patterns and keep the nervous system engaged. Structure: 5 min easy walk to warm up, then: 10 goblet squats (light), 10 wall balls at easy pace, 200m ski erg easy, 200m row easy, 5 burpees at half speed. Cool down with 5 min walk. You should finish feeling energised and snappy, not fatigued. If anything feels heavy or sluggish, stop immediately — the fitness is already banked. Continue carb-loading. Sleep 8+ hours.

Full rest. Walk, stretch gently, eat well, sleep early. Continue carb-loading — this is the most important day for glycogen storage. Lay out race-day kit: shoes (broken in, not new), socks, shorts, shirt, watch (charged), any grip aids for farmer's carry (chalk, gloves). Confirm event logistics — travel time, parking, check-in time, wave start time. Review your April 18 simulation data one final time — set realistic pacing targets for each run leg and station.

Full rest. Final preparation day. Confirm nutrition plan: eat a familiar carb-rich meal 2–3 hours before your start time (e.g., rice + chicken, pasta + sauce, oatmeal + banana). Have a banana or gel 30 min before start. Sip water — don't overhydrate. Pack a post-race recovery meal (protein shake + banana, or sandwich). Review your simulation splits from April 18 and set conservative targets: aim to negative-split the run legs (start at 6:00/km, work down to 5:30/km by the final legs). Mantra for race day: start conservative, build through the middle, give everything on the last two stations. Aim for 8+ hours sleep tonight — this is the most important night of sleep in the entire plan.

OPTIONAL: 10 min very light jog at pure conversational pace, just enough to loosen up and settle pre-race nerves. If you feel good and rested, skip this entirely — full rest is equally valid and arguably better. Do NOT do any intensity. No stretching to new ranges of motion. If your event is morning, rehearse your pre-race meal timing today (eat the same meal at the same time gap). Hydrate well throughout the day but taper fluid intake 2 hours before bed to avoid disrupted sleep. Bed as early as possible. Tomorrow is race day — the work is done. Trust the process.
